comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is almost here, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.2 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week.  This was my sixtieth published book, the second volume of the sprawling study course in Druidical occultism I assembled from fragments, scraps, and a lot of William Walker Atkinson's more obscure writings, as chronicled in the intro to last week's Magic Monday. The Lesser Mysteries comprise four grades, the Greater Mysteries three more, finishing up with the grade of Gwyddon Rhydd or Free Loremaster, which brings with it the right to teach the system. There's a modest but significant number of Free Loremasters running around these days, and some of them do in fact teach; most students of the Dolmen Arch, however, simply learn it from the two books. One way or another, if you're interested, you can find copies for sale here in the US and at your favorite online or brick-and-mortar bookseller elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no more comments will be put through. See you all next week!***

replies.content:
Your divinations might still be giving accurate readings, if your questions were particularly broad. Their short-term outcomes could align quite well with your interests, while their longer-term consequences could be dramatically opposed to your interests. Depending upon how your will and soul interpret your conscious ponderings, your divinations could easily be providing answers to the broadest interpretations of your questions and intents. To find out if something like that is taking place, try breaking your inquiries down into several more limited queries, such as "would this align with my short-term interests?", "are there any long-term drawbacks?", and "will this end up having more downsided than benefits?"

Should that line of questioning yield more reliable readings, then you would come away with more nuanced data by which to plan your best course of action. Should divination reveal that your parents' resistance to or resentment of your autonomy were sufficiently detrimental, you might want to consider taking some protective actions, like getting a P.O. Box to receive any mail that might upset them, or opening a new email account that you never log onto from computers they can access.

Should more bite-sized divinations yield no better results than before, then the task before you is more challenging. That would point to your will being deeply divided. Most certainly, your parents don't have a greater influence over the life force than the gods do (in other words, divination works.) However, your parents may well have that potent of an influence over you and your subconscious. Whatever energetic parasites they have succeeded in implanting in you could easily be distorting your divinatory efforts by making you too afraid to ask, too distracted to hear, or too doutful to trust the wisdom if the divine.

Magic often feels like someone whammying us from outside, but we have to have the vulnerabilities inside of us for that to work in the first place. Clear away all those parasites that are not you, and only your own energies will be left to shine through in their divine truth. Have a blast scouring yourself clean!

— Christophe

Last night I moved into a new apartment, and today less than 24 hours later, I was burglarized. Despite it being a very safe area (residents say they can never remember any thefts happening here in all their years), a thief came in through the window and made off with over $5,000 worth of cash, a gun, backpacks, etc. The dagger was that one of the backpacks, my one and only travel bag for the past 15 years or so with much sentimental value, also contained nearly all of my Druidical paraphernalia.

I am wondering if there is anything I can do magically to recover the Druid stuff, at least. I’ll let the cash, the backpacks, and the gun go. I am in the Druid grade of the CGD, but the kicker is I now can’t open a temple. I lost all of my altar supplies, even the tablecloth, a consecrated wand and sickle, the raw material egg for my serpent’s egg, all of my incense, a bracelet I’d made for use during Merlin’s Wheel, my tarot deck, and a bunch of other things. Literally all I have left is my geomantic wands and the rosary to Ceridwen that I made. Based on the first go round, it would take me months to reacquire and consecrate all this stuff, so I’m pretty dejected, and trying hard not to take this as an omen that the Druid Grade is not meant for me. It feels like such an unusual thing to happen. The apartment is way in the back of a complex with one entry, in a small town, where there is no foot traffic, so it seems like it was a targeted attack by someone watching me moving stuff in.

Of course I’ve filed a police report and there is security footage from a neighbor’s camera of a young woman in her 20’s or early 30’s walking out my patio with an armload of my stuff, but the neighbors don’t recognize her. It doesn’t look good for recovering my things without some kind of divine intervention. Any suggestions? I can still cast a shield chart to find out any further information I might need.

That does leave plenty of non-material interventions, of course.

replies.content:
A Debbie Downer? In what possible way? Your soul is so deeply satisfied with your spiritual growth that it is letting you know that you’re ready to move beyond any crutches you once needed. Now that you’ve learned how to run free, what can crutches do for you but get in your way?

That theft sounded right disappointing up until you discovered that your soul even left a guidepost for you on the windowsill. Given the peculiarly limiting English convention that we only refer to the sill of doorways as thresholds, I’d say your soul has sent you a very powerful message:

Someone watching your behavior noticed your abundance of riches and transgressed a threshold to try to take them from you. That watcher may not have had any idea that she was actually doing your soul’s bidding, but your soul has made quite certain that you would know. You’ve reached the point in your spiritual practice where learning to let go of cherished artifacts is the key to unlocking magical thresholds you hadn’t realized you were ready to cross. That same key will also allow you to relock those magical thresholds to any malign influences wanting to trespass.

You appear to have gotten the resistant watchers within you scared enough to try to steal away your gifts in order to stop your spiritual growth. Foolish watchers, why can’t they ever figure out that their attacks always leave us stronger? Where will you be able to explore now that you no longer need to bring so many crutches with you?

Every rock, stick, and leaf offers a potent portal to divinity. They can be consecrated as and where needed, and then left on the forest floor as a blessing for all who will pass by. Perhaps your soul is trying to let you know that you’ve already reached the point where remaking your lost gear needs little work or expense, as the tools you need are already within you.

- Christophe

replies.title: Re: End of Year Lookback and Anything Learned/Excited about for Past/New Year?
replies.content: I ended up with a great deal of extra time this year due to my wife's death, and one of the things I did to keep the time from dragging was increase the number and range of practices I did. Much of the work will end up in future books. I have been doing a great deal of practice with physiological alchemy, drawing on the broader tradition I touched on very briefly in The Secret of the Golden Section; I have set aside a room in my apartment as a temple space with agalmata (magically consecrated images) of three Druid gods; I've also been putting a lot of work into the Gnostic teachings of Richard Duc de Palatine, and will be formally initiated into his lineage later this year. I taught the first of a series of classes on the Modern Order of Essenes and will be doing more teaching and lecturing in the year ahead. So it should be pretty busy.

replies.title: Re: End of Year Lookback and Anything Learned/Excited about for Past/New Year?

1. What is the purpose of agalmata?

2. Can agalmata be made using non-Druid deities?

replies.title: Re: End of Year Lookback and Anything Learned/Excited about for Past/New Year?
replies.content: Agalmata are a late Neoplatonist invention -- you can find them discussed in some of Proclus's writings, and also in the Hermetic literature in the Asclepius. Since the old temples were being looted and burnt by Christian mobs at that time, Pagans had to work out ways to replace the ancient images of the gods, which had beeen worshipped for centuries and had a great deal of spiritual energy in them. Agalmata were the answer. An agalma is a divine image that's filled with things that resonate with the divine influence invoked into the image -- for example, solar stones, herbs, resins, etc. in an image of Apollo, Mercurial objects in an image of Hermes, and so on. They can be created and used for any deity you like -- Neoplatonism is syncretic, and can be adapted to pretty much any tradition.

replies.title: Re: End of Year Lookback and Anything Learned/Excited about for Past/New Year?
replies.content: That's very interesting. Most of my deity statues are solid, and not easily hollowed out. Can one make an agalma by fixing the statue to a hollow base that is filled with appropriate resonating materials? Is there a specific ritual or set of rituals one must use when creating an agalma?

replies.title: Re: End of Year Lookback and Anything Learned/Excited about for Past/New Year?
replies.content:
1) I don't know whether this would work or not. This is an ancient technique that I'm reviving on the base of very limited textual evidence; it's all experimental, and so trying the hollow base would be a worthwhile experiment.

2) Put the synthemata (the magically effective substances) into the agalma during the day and hour of the ruling planet; that seems to work quite well.

replies.title: Re: End of Year Lookback and Anything Learned/Excited about for Past/New Year?
replies.content:
This seems to be synchronous with the Egyptian/French side of the temple tech - the substance, particularly the shape and design is not an astral symbol of something - it's a direct representation of a higher plane, and resonates with it.

If the neoplatonists had a sense of that approach to magic then this is a profound revelation to me.

replies.title: Re: End of Year Lookback and Anything Learned/Excited about for Past/New Year?
replies.content: I recommend reading the Asclepius, then, and looking into the Neoplatonist approach to worship -- Iamblichus is your starting place here.

replies.title: Re: End of Year Lookback and Anything Learned/Excited about for Past/New Year?
replies.content:
1) Does our limited evidence indicate whether an agalma must be a particular type of divine image (statue, icon, etc.)? I am thinking that I might attach images of the deity to the hollowed base, essentially creating a whole new image that holds up the deity's statue.

2) I am in possession of a Jung relic. I am wondering if that might not also up the potency of a Carl Jung agalma? At least within Catholicism, relics have long been venerated as objects of great spiritual power. That said, I also know that classical polytheists and all the modern ones I know (well, besides me!) don't find the practice very appealing. Not sure what kind of overlap might be possible in this particular case.

And thanks very much for mentioning agalmata. For some reason, I find this to be deeply fascinating, and something that I feel a strong desire to actually do.

replies.title: Re: End of Year Lookback and Anything Learned/Excited about for Past/New Year?
replies.content:
1) I know of no reason to think agalmata were limited in form. Mine are deliberately simple, pieces of tree with the images of Druid deities carved on them in a style that would have fit in very well in northwestern Europe in 1000 BC, and so far have worked very well. In the classic Neoplatonist style, I burn incense before them and offer wheaten cakes and pure water, and recite prayers. The results are good.

2) An agalma is what you use when you don't have relics. The classic Catholic practice that requires at least two relics in an altar on which Mass is performed comes straight out of old Pagan practice, specifically the worship of heroes. (Heroes, remember, are the Greek Pagan equivalent of saints, human beings who on account of their great deeds and personal power were raised by the gods above the human level and, after their deaths, continue to benefit the rest of us.) If you have a relic of Jung you can set up a heroon (pronounced "hero-on") or hero shrine in the classic style, and invoke Jung's spirit with incense and libations.

3) Glad to hear it. I'm working on a book on the practical application of Neoplatonist teachings to modern polytheism, and agalmata will feature in it.

comments.title: Planetary Debilities
comments.content:
Good evening!

Last Magic Monday you linked an article on calculating planetary dignities and debilities using William Lilly's numerical factor tables. I went over my natal chart using the tables and found all planets relatively decently dignified (about 10-20 range), except for Jupiter, which is debilitated to the score of -11 and placed in a rather consequential house. If I understand it correctly, in Lilly's system an ill dignified planet can be a significant source of negative influence. Is there a protocol for rectifying this kind of influence?

replies.title: Re: Planetary Debilities
replies.content:
Not in Western astrological magic -- the standard approach there is to rely on the well-dignified planets in your chart. In Vedic astrology, however, planetary charity -- making offerings to the intelligences of the planets via charity -- is a common practice, and I know of a fair number of Western astrological mages who have had good results with it. Instructions are here:

https://www.renaissanceastrology.com/planetarycharity.html

replies.title: Re: Planetary Debilities
replies.content: Can you do planetary charity with Uranus or Neptune?

replies.title: Re: Planetary Debilities
replies.content: Presumably, but you'd have to work out what charities are appropriate for them.

comments.title: If I may ask a few questions about the circumstances in which you stopped watching television ...
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

comments.content:
I have a strong intuitive sense that Hermes has chosen to offer me guidance and patronage. I have a few questions about that:

1. Has a deity ever approached you like this?

2. What’s with Hermes’ vast attributes of overseeing magic and alchemy and learning and writing etc etc (all noble and civilizing enterprises) but then he’s also the god of trickery? What do you make of the reputation for trickery?

replies.title: Re: Hermes/Thoth
replies.content:
1) Yes. You have the choice to accept or decline.

2) All these things are also contexts for trickery. Writing is the basis for fake credentials and deceptive propaganda; learning is as often as not learning how to cheat; fraudulent alchemists are more common than honest ones, and the ability to convince the mind in the absence of facts is a basic element of many kins of magic. Mercury is the god of ambivalence, of things that can be used for good and evil alike.

3) No. Thoth is a moon god; Hermes corresponds to the planet Mercury. Thoth doesn't have the reputation for trickery, either.

comments.title: The failure of spirituality
comments.content:
JMG,

This a bit of a follow up to my question about astrology last week. In this week alone I heard two people with an online presence and following make quite remarkable statements that at least to my understanding seems a bit deluded. Both of them do channeling of various ascended beings and one of them actually claims to read the Akashic Records like an open book. What is remarkable about this is that it seems like the mainstream media and the liberal/left side of the political spectrum must be channeling the same ascended beings and reading the same books in the Akashic library.
During the last five years I repeatedly found myself being disappointed with various spiritually inclined people, some whom I know and some that I dont. Whether its yogic practicioners, astrologers or spiritual channelers most of them seem to be almost completely subsumed by the popular narratives. Why is this? Why does their respective spiritual practices fail to inspire to wisdom, or at least critical thinking? That is at least what I used to think was part of the goal with these various traditions. I am not myself involved in any of the aforementioned practices, but in another magical/spiritual tradition native to where I am from, a tradition that focuses a lot on personal empowerment and responsibility.
I am not an occultist but I get the feeling that people within the broader occult traditions have generally faired better during the last few years and have not to the same degree been consumed by the mainstream madness. Is that your feeling also? If so, what do you think the reason for this is? And what would be the most important component of a spiritual system of practice that inspires to wisdom and an ability to not get swept away by the narratives?

/Fredrik

replies.title: Re: The failure of spirituality
replies.content:
First of all, you're quite right. A lot of people who think they're in touch with profound spiritual wisdom are mindlessly parroting the messages of the corporate media. The last decade has been an acid test for the ability of spiritual traditions to help people rise above the mass mind, and a great many of the traditions active in the Western world have failed that test utterly, often in the most humiliating ways.

I do have a theory about why this is, though it'll doubtless offend some people. The forms of meditation practiced in most spiritual traditions in the modern West teach people to shut down the thinking mind. Whether they're chanting mantras, practicing mindfulness meditation, or what have you, they're all teaching themselves not to think, and it's not surprising that in fact, they've learned not to think.

Classic Western occultism, by contrast, teaches discursive meditation, which engages the thinking mind. In discursive meditation you don't silence your thoughts, nor do you observe them floating past while making no attempt to assess them -- you examine them, study them, take them apart to see how they work, and put them back together in various ways. That develops skills that enable the practitioner to see through the kind of cheap thoughtstoppers marketed by the corporate media. There may be other tools that make this happen, but discursive meditation is the one I know.

replies.title: Re: The failure of spirituality
replies.content:
Thank you for your toughts, this issue has been on my mind for a while. Maybe it could partly be that many of the popular alternatives to the western mainstream religion are also mainstream religions, but people dont see it because the traditions in question are from foreign cultures? Maybe the esoteric traditions from those cultures would fare better?

Discursive meditation sounds interesting, how do you systematize it? What would be a good wtitten source for this? One of your books?

/Fredrik

replies.title: Re: The failure of spirituality
replies.content:
1) It really depends on the tradition. Some branches of Buddhism, for example, traditionally teach logic to monks and have them study the Buddhist scriptures to get their brains working well; others don't.

2) I've covered it in nearly all of my books of magical instruction. The most detailed discussion so far is in The Druidry Handbook; I'm planning on an entire book on the subject fairly soon.

replies.title: Re: The failure of spirituality
replies.content: I'm reminded of the way that the Neoplatonists would start by working through Aristotle-- and that by beginning with the works on logic-- before approaching Plato's more mystical works. That also would have meant that you wouldn't be doing the mind-emptying meditative practices taught by e.g. Plotinus until you had a very solid grounding in logic and reason.

replies.title: Re: The failure of spirituality
replies.content: Yep. They knew what they were doing.

replies.title: Re: The failure of spirituality
replies.content:
My understanding is that proper Buddhist practice is about studying the sutras and developing your mind, using mind-clearing exercises for rest and as a palate-cleanser; this is analogous to athletes alternating between workouts and recovery periods.
The mindfulness meditation that's popular in the West is a watered-down, stripped-down, pasteurized version of Buddhism, meant to lull people into complacency with the fuzzy relaxing stuff, all nuanced philosophy & critical thinking excised, since that might lead them to reject their servitude.

replies.title: Re: The failure of spirituality
replies.content: Buddhism is a diverse faith, and it includes more and less intellectual expressions. In some sects, yes, mind-clearing exercises are a palate-cleanser; in others, they're more than that -- but they're never the be-all and end all. You're right, though, that it's only in the bowdlerized "Western Buddhism" so heavily marketed these days by, and to, the clueless that you see the focus on mindlessness become the be-all and end-all of the tradition.

replies.title: Re: The failure of spirituality
replies.content: Teaching the non-engagement with thoughts is a core part of cognitive behavioural therapy, which is in effect, clinical stoicism (the developer of CBT built it from Epictetus). What just occurred to me is that the same corporate, middle class types who are prescribed CBT for a range of things, are also prescribed non-thinking mindfulness meditation. So they've been prescribed opposites, and no wonder there's a great deal of mental distress.

comments.title: Magic for the Wilderness Outdoorsmen
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

Recently I have been developing a strong interest in back woods wilderness hunting. There are three elements of this that relate to magic, and I would like to know if you can point me in the right direction to sources that detail what I should or should not be doing, magically speaking, while in the deep woods.

The first thing involves offerings to the land. I know Native Americans used to do things like offer tobacco before the hunt. That no longer seems relevant in a world where the product is dirt cheap, and honestly, I don't want to support the tobacco industry anyway. Can I substitute something like removing garbage from the land scape near where I live (although I don't know how far the reach of that activity would span)? Or a different sacrifice? They allegedly grew so close to the land that animals would appear and allow themselves to be slain. Presumably this is not because the land "needed" tobacco, but rather because the consciousness directing a particular species was impressed by a sincere desire to respect the land. That's all well and good but figuring out how to appease these immaterial minds is less than straight forward. Well, at least it is for 21st century folks like me.

The second thing is much more defensive than relational. The deep woods is the last bastion for large scale cryptic activity. You are probably also aware that thousands of people go missing in our national parks, and that there is a correlation between regions with underground cave networks and large numbers of missing persons. This creates an interesting problem. If there are beings that are killing or kidnapping humans who spend a lot of time underground one has to wonder how to protect oneself. Are there protection rituals specially designed to deal with these kinds of threats? Or do general protection spells work just fine?

Finally, is there a substantial difference between protective prayer vs magical ritual?

Thank you for your time.

replies.title: Re: Magic for the Wilderness Outdoorsmen
replies.content:
Not JMG, but two books I recommend as research for your interests:

The Navajo and the Animal People: Native American Traditional Ecological Knowledge and Ethnozoology by Steve Pavlik and William B Tsosie

Giving Voice to Bear: North American Indian Rituals, Myths, and Images of the Bear by David B. Rockwell

Lots of very interesting details and anecdotes in these books. Pavlik is a scholar/professor/academic but very much on an “Ecosophia” wavelength.

replies.title: Re: Magic for the Wilderness Outdoorsmen
replies.content:
All this is a field that has barely been touched in classic Western occultism. Any suggestions I can offer will be a matter of personal experience and theory, and should be treated as that, not as hard and fast rules.

1) I have found that nature spirits are very appreciative when a human picks up trash, or does anything else direct to decrease negative human impacts on nature. They also talk to one another; if you pour water on the roots of a tree close to your home during the hot summer months, when that's the equivalent of handing somebody hot and thirsty a good cold beer, tree spirits far away will know of it.

2) You'd be well advised to check local folklore. Hostile subterranean beings are of course common in the world's traditions, but there's a lot of variation from place to place about what chases them off.

replies.title: Re: Magic for the Wilderness Outdoorsmen
replies.content:
I wonder if it has to be Nicotiana tabacum? There are several species of Nicotiana (usually sold as “flowering tobacco”) that produce beautiful flowers for garden or vase, and some varieties of which have a lovely scent and will attract moths and other pollinators to your garden in the evenings. Perhaps these would be acceptable—could be worth a try.

FWIW, when I’ve lost something in the woods, I have had good luck asking the nature spirits for help and then starting to pick up trash. I usually find the lost object and then pick up at least one more piece of trash with my thanks.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ancient Babylon used a base-60 number system, with very intuitive notation -- the subradices were 10 & 6, so you'd draw angles/boomerangs for tens (0-5), and then marks/lines for 1's (1-9), so the number itself told you its value. Base-60, while large, has a lot of powerful qualities as a superior highly composite number, and the number system was able to break down the bulk, but that's going off on a tangent.
Point being, the ancient use of base-60 seems to have influenced timekeeping (60s/60m/12,24h) and angles/geometry (360 degrees). Thus, it's very plausible that the days of the week were also formulated back in the Bronze Age.

comments.title: Diagram tradition in esotericism
comments.content:
I thought the following might be of interest to some people here.

I recently came across a book by the art historian Conrad Rudolph called The Mystic Ark. Actually, it's two books -- a small paperback (First I Find the Center Point: Reading the Text of Hugh of Saint Victor's “The Mystic Ark” Transactions, American Philosophical Society, vol. 94), and a fairly massive translation of and commentary on The Mystic Ark text (The Mystic Ark: Hugh of Saint Victor, Art, and Thought in the Twelfth Century, Cambridge University Press, 2014).

Both are not cheap, especially the latter, but worth a look if one can get them through a library.

There's a web site at https://mysticark.ucr.edu/. Conrad Rudolph also worked with a team to re-create the original diagrams (https://mysticark.ucr.edu/the-illustrations/mystic-ark-diagrams/) There's a lot more on the web site. and some of the diagrams are gorgeous, and well worth a look. The diagrams were actually printed out, wall-sized, for a museum exhibit, and there's a nice picture of Rudolph standing in front of the mural (http://mysticark.ucr.edu/wp-content/uploads/largefiles/Fig-22-4096.jpg, and https://mysticark.ucr.edu/the-illustrations/mystic-ark-diagrams/). The image files are all available for downloading, by the way.

There are several points that the author/editor/translator doesn't cover, but that are probably interesting to us.

Rudolph strongly criticizes Mary Carruthers' claim that The Mystic Ark is an example of an art of memory system, and in a certain sense he's right, in that it's not a set of place-holders to be used in the art of memory, but a fully-stocked database that was meant to accompany and contain the contents of lecture-series dealing with the ark of Noah as a kind of summary of history, theology, and spirituality. A modern analogy (though an impoverished one) would be a power-point deck made to accompany a set of live lectures.

But, on the other hand, it is a memory engine of the sort Carruthers describes so well, in that it allows the user to held "in a single vision" a complex manifold of material -- and deal with it creatively.

Briefly, the main diagram was an attempt to link four levels of reality, the history and structure of the cosmos and the microcosm, and the story of Fall and Reintegration. It was used as a way of maintaining mental focus -- and for a lecture series that laid out how to understand and use the diagrams and all the collected lore. The images were supposed to be very striking and inspiring, richly colored, and full of information, tying everything into a common structure.

Most interesting, perhaps, is that the lecture series was presented at "dinners" at which members of the monastery or college would listen, look, and discuss. It was intended to help rise up the levels from the material and historical to the spiritual and transformative. The diagrams and texts were meant to be (and were) copied and presented elsewhere by other lecturers, in the context of group meetings and discussions.

(Current esotericists are actually familiar with something very similar, but using the Tree of Life -- a very simple diagram that can be richly elaborated (for example, with sephirotic and telesmatic images, and so on). In other words, the Tree of Life, or similar diagrams, could be used in the same way. And of course the Tree does contain a vast amount of lore -- but with a large, mural-sized version, it could be quite the center for talks and discussions.)

A final point. For the Mystic Ark diagram, substitute a "trestleboard" diagram, with its associated lecture. Imagine exhibiting the trestleboard as a large mural in the context of a light dinner, or brown-bag lunch, to a group of initiates, and, after the lecture, opening the floor for discussion, in a kind of group discursive meditation.

Is it not possible that something like the monastic or collegial practice exemplified by the Mystic Ark material managed to find its way into the practices of fraternal initiatory organizations, and so continue on, in diminished form, even to the present?

Smaragdine Senex

replies.title: Re: Diagram tradition in esotericism
replies.content:
1) Many thanks for this! I'm familiar with Hugh of St. Victor's system, and with the academic quarrels around it -- the squabble's rooted as usual in inadequate attention to definitions and preconceptions.

2) Not only possible but likely. Fraternal initiatory organizations are descended from medieval guilds, which took their organizational structure and many of their other practices from medieval religious confraternities -- and Hugh of St. Victor would have been a very logical resource for confraternities to use.

replies.title: Re: Diagram tradition in esotericism
replies.content:
Yes, "Many are the arguments of scholars"!

I suspect Abbé Louis Constant, both in his education and his work on Migne's Patrologia, as well as his contact with the émigré occultists of the "Great Emigration", was very aware of the ecclesiastical channels through which the esoteric traditions flowed. Not explicitly mentioning that background was probably part of his art of silence.

In an amusing sense, one of the biggest secrets of the Secret Tradition is the extent to which it was not particularly secret, and in fact was largely publicly shared, through the middle ages.

Smaragdine Senex

replies.title: Re: Diagram tradition in esotericism
replies.content:
By the way, the travels of a member of the Czarnacki family came up a while back: I'd be delighted to see something of that show up on an Ariel Moravec story. There might also be something there in connection with the Polish Haitian community -- for example, the influence images of the Black Madonna of Częstochowa on the iconography of Ezili Dantor, and perhaps some eastern European Martinism as well.

All the best,

Smaragdine Senex

replies.content:

https://www.ecosophia.net/blogs-and-essays/the-well-of-galabes/how-not-to-learn-magic-an-introductory-note/

replies.title: Re: Julius Evola
replies.content: In that post, you mention the concept of the “tainted sphere” (I’m guessing a problematic egregor?) and say you’ll discuss it in subsequent posts, but when I search I don’t turn up anything. Do you remember if/where you ever elaborated on it further?

replies.title: Re: Julius Evola
replies.content: I honestly don't recall if I did or not. The basic concept's simple enough, though. A group that works with spiritual energies, if it falls into some set of bad habits and does nothing to reform itself, will establish patterns of consciousness in its egregor that will cause those same bad habits to repeat indefinitely, and those remain in place even if the original organization falls apart -- any other organization that uses the same rituals and symbols will get the same habit. That's why Golden Dawn order are so reliably plagued with egotistical power struggles -- the sphere of the GD is tainted. To avoid the taint you have to change the rituals and symbolism thoroughly -- which is why some of the GD offshoots have done just fine.

replies.title: Re: Julius Evola
replies.content: Thanks! That’s about what I figured.

replies.title: Re: Julius Evola
replies.content: It occurs to me that this is one of the reasons why Neopaganism is in decline, and it will not be easy to do some constructive thing about it because the concept of a tainted sphere osn't known to many people.

replies.title: Re: Julius Evola

comments.title: Pattern Magic/Mysticism
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Hopefully, this is somewhat cogent.

I have been integrating with my own organically developing occult practice key ideas from Robert Mathiesen's recently published books. I have been experimenting with "emptying" myself (in a somewhat apophatic style) and "going beyond" to the essence of whatever I am contemplating, very much as I used to do at an etheric level with energy work. I will at times experience a kind of dual perspective. A sharing of perspective between myself and an other. Could be a book, cloud, whatever. Sometimes done in meditation other times out walking. A dual non-duality maybe? Physical and mental distance for example are no longer meaningful.

It appears to also happen with relationships or connections between things. The relationship between a book and my watch for example. Or a sailboat mast and a house on an overlooking bluff. The relationship itself being as much a thing to experience this dual non-duality with as the individual objects.

This got me thinking about how much of my life has been about working with patterns. Healthcare data analysis, math, programming, etc.... Even astrology finally opened to me once I began looking at an ephemeris as a source of data filled with number patterns.

What happens in a model where reality (or at least the reality I am capable of sensing in some manner) is a web and I am a node in that web? My inner work intensifies or grows in some manner the node that I am. Work with the other nodes in the web and even the patterns of connections themselves as entities in their own right might grow stronger. Effecting change in myself rippling out along the patterns of the web of creation.

So many patterns! Infinitely many!

Out of these experiences, my practice is evolving. More accurately, maybe, my approach to my practice is evolving. Emptying and going beyond when contemplating astrology themes for example has been quite exciting. Likely, the same can be done with alchemy, sacred geometry, knots, weaving, writing, tarot, and much more.

I don't expect that these are novel or unique realizations. As such I am curious about others works in this direction. Anything or anyone you or others might point me towards?

Thanks,
Eric

replies.content: I haven't read extensively in the literature of this sort of mysticism and so don't have any recommendations to offer. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Pattern Magic/Mysticism
replies.content:
This writing reminds a lot of my experiences with Buddhism, the perception of “śunyata”, and my work under a high Tibetan Lama. The Tibetans also believe all things are connected, like a cosmic web.

The Buddhists as well use that concept of connectedness — we are at one with the things around us — to practice their inseparableness with the qualities of the Bodhisattvas: unity with their compassion and loving-kindness, etc

replies.title: Re: Pattern Magic/Mysticism
replies.content: What you are saying sounds very reminiscent of "Indra's Net", if you have not encountered that before, though I don't know much about the literature directly about it.

replies.title: Re: Pattern Magic/Mysticism
replies.content: Hi Eric, would you mind sharing the title of the book you had in mind? A quick internet search didn't show any hits for recent titles by Robert Mathiesen.

replies.title: Re: Pattern Magic/Mysticism
replies.content:

Principia Magica
&
Microcosmographia Magica

replies.title: Re: Pattern Magic/Mysticism
replies.content:
This kind of perspective appeals to me as well. I was hoping to find some pre-existing description of an ideal for practice relating to it. But the closest thing I've heard about it in established disciplines is the individualistic ideas of "ipsissimus" in the Golden Dawn traditions (and a related idea from Christian devotional practice about people who come closer to their divine image becoming more distinct, with a metaphor of cleaning a stained glass window). I was imagining something sort of like a facilitator, and sort of like a market-maker, and sort of like a censor (in what I loosely understand to be the Roman sense).

It has sometimes seemed to me as though shamanic-style practice would be more appropriate to pursuing such an ideal. But, perhaps not coincidentally, I've faced a chicken-and-egg problem of not being in an already-existing position in a web of relations where I had a trusted concept of what kinds of traditions, practices, or entities to trust.

I also don't see much valorization of shamanism around here, and (skipping some steps for time) it makes me wonder if maybe I'm just trying to run away from the problem of needing to get myself in order before I can be a point of pivoting on which any other patterns, that the parts of the pattern called "myself are also bound up in", can be put in order.

I've read about something called the Vision of Splendor, associated with the sphere of Hod, which sounds sort of like what you're talking about. (It was Colin Low's "Notes on Kabbalah"; I haven't read much about Kabbalah actually.) Which leads into the possibility that it might be important to then go on to cultivate the ability to also contact the headspace of some of the other spheres' Visions, for balance, especially Netzach. An important distinction between Hod and Netzach, as I understand it, is that Hod leans more towards legible patterns which can be understood as compositions of a small number of refined, more-interchangable components similar to letters or atoms, where Netzach leans towards illegible patterns where many things are connected to many other things in largely obscure, not-yet-refined or refinement-resistant ways, and the habits needed to keep afloat are quite different.

If you've a mind for it, you might want to learn about some of the scientific and mathematical disciplines relating to patterns and metapatterns:

- complex systems science, which leads into ecology, parts of linguistics, and statistical field theory

- computer science -- mostly the idea of Turing-universality, which leads into the Solomonoff-Kolmogorov-Chaitin-Levin concepts of parsimony and universal inductive reasoning for patterns with perfectly isolated explanations; there is also an undeveloped field of inquiry about metapatterns describing approximate similitudes of patterns not isolated from their lower-level details, which could be derived from Garrabrant's concept of inductive probability for logical truths

(The computer science ones are probably the closest you can get to an imagined version of Indra's net that doesn't just collapse into reflecting your preconceptions back at you as soon as you try to develop its imagined implications to any significant degree unassisted. Universal algorithmic probability is very explicit about where it has your preconceptions come in, and then it mostly focuses on implications that are relevant under any reasonable set of preconceptions.)

- the parts of mathematics that are about relations of mathematical entities to each other, including when those entities are themselves relations of mathematical entities to each other -- category theory mostly, but also higher-order logic and homotopy type theory

You might also benefit from reading some of Hofstadter's earlier books -- Godel, Escher, Bach and Metamagical Themas.

comments.title: Proofreader/editor needed for Sacred Homemaking

replies.title: Re: Proofreader/editor needed for Sacred Homemaking
replies.content: In general, there are different levels of editing from developmental (you have ideas but need help fleshing them out and structuring them) to line editing (improving prose line by line) to proofreading (just looking for mechanical errors) and everything in between. Different editors will specialize in different types of editing and different stages of the editing process (proofreading after line editing, etc). If author and editor have different expectations about the type of editing, it can be a problem. Do you know what level of editing you’re looking for? Big-picture feedback on content and structure, or improving readability and clarity of the prose, or just catching any remaining grammar/punctuation errors?

comments.title: Clearing Bad Energy from Neglected Space
comments.content:
JMG - This is a follow up to a question I asked several months ago. Over the past several years, my home office has fallen into a terrible state for various reasons. I have neglected doing any cleaning of it (dusting, vacuuming, etc), and it has become the storage location for anything that doesn't have an obvious location elsewhere in the house. I spend a lot of time in there, as I work from home and perform my job in there. It's also the room in the house that is purely mine (all my books, music, art collection, etc. are in there).

Over this time, I have felt a major shift in energy in the room. The last time I overhauled it, it was almost a dream room for me. Very clean and tidy, with all my books and art. Over the course of the deterioration, it has felt heavy, full of dread, and I've had a sense of parasitic type entities in there feeding off the grossness (disorderliness, dust, cobwebs, etc.).

I have spent the past two weeks overhauling it - cleaning thoroughly, discarding things I don't want, incorporating new books, art, music into their proper places. I have two questions.

1. Going-forward, is there anything additional I should do to shift the energy and clear out anything unwanted?

2. On the 3rd day of this overhaul process, I developed a terrible sciatic pain on my right side. I have never had this issue before. I can't think of anything physical that would have caused it, though I do lift weights (so can't rule that out). The pain has been severe. I know some physical therapy and have been able to do some routines that improve things for 2-4 hours at a time until it returns, at which point I do another routine, etc.

I found some things online (unknown credibility) indicating that sciatica is esoterically related to imbalances and in particular: "The right side of the body is the masculine half, relating to yang energy. This is the energy of taking action, getting things done, thinking rationally, and emotions like anger and frustration."

I have found that it has tended to trigger worse when I encounter a particularly difficult part of the office overhaul (extra messy area or some complicated set of dependencies). Do you have any thoughts about this? Could it be emotionally self-inflicted? Could it be an attack from the parasitic entities I have sensed in the room in the past?

Thanks for your help.

replies.title: Re: Clearing Bad Energy from Neglected Space
replies.content:
1) The first thing you need to do is figure out where the negative energy came from. Journaling is one good way to do that. Ask yourself where the negativity came from, and then let yourself write down whatever comes out. Do not censor or edit at all. What you get may not be literally true but it will lead you to understanding.

2) The same rule applies here, though making sure you get extravagant amounts of rest and sleep is also important. Nerve issues such as sciatica very often have emotional origins -- and you have to find and experience the emotions to release them.
